content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 507 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                                322-5601                        322-6441

March 5, 1976


Michael Wornum
Assemblyman, 9th District
21 Tamál Vista Blvd. #195
Corte Madera, Ca. 94925

                                                                Re: Request for Opinion -
                                                                    No. 76-018

Dear Assemblyman Wornum:

     Thank you for your letter of February 6, 1976, requesting
an opinion with respect to whether a post office box can be used
instead of a street address when complying with the mass mailing
provisions of the Political Reform Act.

     Since your request does not pose a substantial question
requiring interpretation of the Act, no formal opinion will be
issued. I hope, however, that the following comments will be
helpful. Section 84305 of the Act expressly requires that, in
sending a mass mailing, the cost of which is reportable, postage
must be paid by postage meter, the mail must be sent by bulk
rate or the sender must include "the sender's name, street
address and city." A post office box is not the equivalent of
a street address and, therefore, does not satisfy the express
requirement of the Act. Accordingly, the post office box can-
not be used instead of a street address.

     The Commission intends, however, to suggest a legislative
amendment to Section 84305 which, in the case of a "committee,"
would permit the use of a street address or a post office box.
I assume this would help to solve the problem to which you allude.

     If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request,
you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal.Adm. Code Section 18321. If you
have any further questions, please contact Kenneth Finney, Chief
of the Commission's Legal Division.

                                                    Sincerely,

                                                    [signature]
                                                    Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                                    Chairman

DHL:pvp

MICHAEL WORNUM
ASSEMBLYMAN, NINTH DISTRICT

February 6, 1976


Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

It has come to my attention that all mass mailings must show
a street address and that a Post Office box is insufficient.

As a permanent address, a Post Office box would seem to me
to be far better than a street address, which not only may
change several times during an election campaign but which
becomes completely non-existent the day following election.

I hereby respectfully request a determination as to whether
or not a Post Office box is acceptable as an address for mass
mailings and an explanation of the rationale used in reaching
this decision.

Thank you for your consideration of this matter. Your prompt
response will be much appreciated.

Sincerely,

Michael Wornum

MW:dhc
cc: Mrs. Olive Lewis

MICHAEL WORNUM
ASSEMBLYMAN, NINTH DISTRICT

January 27, 1976


Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 507
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

Attached you will find a copy of a January 15th letter from
James E. Jensen, Director of Governmental Affairs, California
State University and Colleges, and a copy of my January 23rd
letter to him.

Please note that he offered, and I accepted, a parking pass
which I plan to use while I am visiting the State College in
my Assembly district. According to Mr. Jensen (and I believe
him), this "gift" must be reported because it may amount to
more than $25.00.

Frankly, I believe the FPPC is completely destroying the
definition of "gift" with ridiculous rulings like this. How
this can be considered a "gift" when it will only be used on
official (i.e. "State") business, I do not know. Although I
was a firm backer of Proposition 9 and have found many good
things have come out of the initiative, some of the inter-
pretations leave a heck of a lot to be desired.

Sincerely,

Michael Wornum

MW:dhd
attachments

SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA
MICHAEL WORNUM

January 23, 1976


Mr. James B. Jensen, Director
Governmental Affairs
California State University and Colleges
5670 Wilshire Blvd.
Los Angeles, California 90036

Dear Mr. Jensen:

Thank you for your letter of January 15th and the enclosure of
the 1976 parking pass. I am quite happy to receive the pass
and plan on visiting the state college in my district as time
permits. I recognize the importance these institutions of higher
learning play in our society and, therefore, plan to keep a
watchful eye on the state campus in my district.

Furthermore, thank you for informing me of the Fair Political
Practices Commission's decision on such issues as this. I will
make all the necessary arrangements to let the Commission know
of my receiving the pass.

Again, let me thank you for your generosity. I hope to make the
best use possible of the pass.

Sincerely,

Michael Wornum

MW:dhb
cc: Ms. Carol Staley

P.S. Wornum it's absolutely ridiculous & unjust that such a 
"privilege" has to be reported just so as a "gift" to help bring to
somebody.

THE CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY AND COLLEGES

5670 WILSHIRE BOULEVARD • LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90036 • (213) 935-2933

THE CHANCELLOR


January 15, 1976


The Honorable Michael Wornum
Member of the State Assembly
State Capitol
Sacramento, California  95814

Dear Assemblyman Wornum:

I am pleased to enclose a 1976 parking pass for your personal
use while on official business at one of our campuses.

It is important that I bring to your attention Report No. 6,
PAN-Political Practices Commission Actions and the August
1975 opinion of the Legislative Counsel of California (#75-25)
which states: "An unsolicited parking pass given to the
State Controller by the Office of Governmental Affairs of
The California State University and Colleges for his use
while on official business at any California State University
or College campus must be reported by the State Controller,
pursuant to Section 87207 (a) (1), as a gift received worth
$25.00 or more in value." Therefore, if you prefer not to
report the pass, please return it and we will remove your
name from our list.

I join with Chancellor Dumke and The California State University and Colleges' Presidents in expressing the hope that
you will retain the parking pass under the condition noted
above and use it often.

Sincerely,

James E. Jensen, Director
Governmental Affairs

JEJ:cr

Enclosure
